past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed at age 22, infrequent episodes (2-3 per year)
Patent foramen ovale identified incidentally on echocardiogram performed during college sports physical at age 19, deemed hemodynamically insignificant, no intervention recommended
Mild intermittent low back pain attributed to sedentary work posture
family_history: Father with hypertension, diagnosed at age 55
Maternal grandmother died of stroke at age 80
No family history of early thrombotic events or connective tissue disorders

social_history.living_situation: Lives with girlfriend in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Regular recreational cycling, 3-4 times per week, 30-45 minutes
social_history.illicit_drugs: Denies, no history of recreational drug use
social_history.recent_travel: Returned from a 14-hour flight from Tokyo 3 days ago for a work conference

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Anxious but cooperative. Speech is fluent without aphasia or dysarthria. Attention and concentration grossly intact. No neglect on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Visual fields full to confrontation. Subtle incomplete right internuclear ophthalmoplegia: impaired adduction of the right eye on leftward gaze with dissociated nystagmus of the left (abducting) eye. Mild left-beating horizontal nystagmus in primary gaze that does not suppress with visual fixation. Vertical nystagmus on upgaze, direction-changing. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. Normal muscle bulk and tone throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased pinprick sensation over the left face (V1-V3 distribution). Decreased pinprick and temperature sensation over the right arm and leg. Proprioception and vibration intact in all extremities. Light touch intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Right finger-to-nose with mild terminal dysmetria. Right heel-to-shin with subtle ataxia. Left-sided coordination intact. Rapid alternating movements mildly impaired on the right.
neurological_exam.gait: Wide-based gait with tendency to veer to the right. Unable to perform tandem gait. Romberg negative with eyes open, positive with eyes closed with fall to the right.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 4. Pronounced nausea with positional changes. Head impulse test is NEGATIVE (no corrective saccade), which is inconsistent with a peripheral vestibular lesion and raises concern for a central cause. HINTS exam: Head Impulse Negative, Nystagmus direction-changing, Test of Skew positive (right hypertropia) — all three components suggest central etiology.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset severe vertigo, nausea, and difficulty walking
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Carlos Reyes is a 35-year-old right-handed man with no significant vascular risk factors who presents to the emergency department via ambulance with acute onset of severe rotational vertigo, nausea, and unsteady gait. The patient reports that he was at his desk at work at approximately 09:15 this morning when he experienced a sudden, intense sensation of the room spinning accompanied by severe nausea. He attempted to stand but immediately felt as though he was falling to the right and had to grab onto his desk to prevent himself from hitting the floor. A coworker found him on the floor moments later, visibly distressed and vomiting repeatedly. He noticed that his vision seemed 'doubled,' particularly when looking to the left, and he felt that objects appeared to be jumping. He also reports a dull, constant headache localized to the back of his head that began at the same time as the vertigo. He denies any hearing loss, tinnitus, or aural fullness. He has not had any recent upper respiratory infection or ear infection. He denies any weakness or numbness in his limbs, though on further questioning he acknowledges that the right side of his body has felt 'slightly odd' and 'tingly,' which he attributed to anxiety. He mentions that he returned from a long-haul flight from Tokyo three days ago and had noticed some mild calf soreness during the trip, which resolved. He has no history of prior episodes of vertigo. He had a migraine approximately one month ago, which presented with throbbing unilateral headache and photophobia but no aura, no vertigo, and resolved with sumatriptan. His girlfriend, who accompanied him, confirms he was in his usual state of health this morning before leaving for work. EMS reports that his symptoms have been persistent and have not fluctuated during transport. He has vomited four times since symptom onset.

findings.location: Right lateral medulla and inferior cerebellar peduncle
findings.size: Approximately 1.4 x 0.8 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.DWI: Hyperintense signal consistent with restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, equivocal given early time window
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in right lateral medulla

findings.borders: Poorly defined margins in the lateral medullary region
Poorly defined, conforming to PICA vascular territory

additional_observations: No evidence of vertebral artery dissection on standard MRI sequences, though dedicated vessel wall imaging not performed
Fourth ventricle is normal in size and configuration, no hydrocephalus
Supratentorial brain parenchyma is unremarkable without evidence of infarction or mass
MRA of the intracranial circulation demonstrates diminished flow signal in the right distal vertebral artery and proximal PICA
mri.impression: 1. Acute infarctions involving the right lateral medulla and right inferior cerebellar hemisphere in the PICA territory, consistent with posterior inferior cerebellar artery territory ischemic stroke. 2. Diminished flow signal in the right distal vertebral artery on MRA raises concern for vertebral artery dissection or severe stenosis — dedicated CTA or vessel wall MRI recommended. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation or hydrocephalus.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right PICA territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction in lateral medulla and inferior cerebellum conforming to PICA vascular territory
Lesion distribution conforms to vascular territory rather than typical periventricular/juxtacortical MS pattern; no other white matter lesions
PRES typically involves bilateral parieto-occipital regions; DWI restriction pattern here favors infarction

recommended_actions: CTA head and neck to evaluate vertebral arteries for dissection
Consider vessel wall MRI if CTA is inconclusive
Echocardiogram with bubble study to evaluate PFO as potential embolic source
Hypercoagulability workup given young age and absence of traditional risk factors

findings: Normal axis, intervals, and waveform morphology
No atrial enlargement or ventricular hypertrophy
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead electrocardiogram. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes identified.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG does not exclude a cardioembolic source. In a young patient with cryptogenic stroke, prolonged cardiac monitoring and echocardiography with bubble study are recommended to evaluate for patent foramen ovale or paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated LDL, though not a primary stroke risk factor in this age group

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Elevated D-dimer. In the context of recent long-haul flight and young stroke, raises concern for paradoxical embolism via PFO or vertebral artery dissection with in-situ thrombosis.

labs.interpretation: Laboratory studies are largely unremarkable with no evidence of infection, inflammation, coagulopathy, diabetes, or significant metabolic derangement. The notably elevated D-dimer at 1.42 mg/L FEU is significant in a young patient with acute stroke and recent prolonged air travel, raising the possibility of deep venous thrombosis with paradoxical embolism through the known PFO, or alternatively may reflect in-situ thrombosis from vertebral artery dissection. Mildly elevated LDL is noted but not a primary contributor. Hypercoagulability workup should be pursued.
abnormal_values_summary: D-dimer 1.42 mg/L FEU (elevated, >2x upper limit of normal)

findings.location: Right vertebral artery, V3 segment (atlas loop)

signal_characteristics.CTA: Eccentric luminal narrowing of the right vertebral artery at the V3 segment with intimal flap and mural thrombus. Tapered irregular stenosis with approximately 70% luminal narrowing over a 2.3 cm segment. Subtle intramural hematoma identified.
signal_characteristics.Lumen: Narrowed and irregular with string sign appearance

findings.location: Right distal vertebral artery and right PICA origin

signal_characteristics.CTA: Decreased opacification of the right distal vertebral artery distal to the dissection and diminished caliber of the right PICA at its origin, consistent with flow-limiting dissection

additional_observations: Left vertebral artery is patent and dominant with normal caliber throughout
Bilateral internal carotid arteries are patent without stenosis or dissection
Basilar artery is patent with normal caliber
No evidence of aneurysm or other vascular malformation
output.impression: 1. Right vertebral artery dissection at the V3 segment with eccentric luminal narrowing, intimal flap, and mural thrombus. Approximately 70% stenosis with string sign appearance. 2. Diminished flow in right distal vertebral artery and right PICA origin, consistent with the mechanism of the posterior circulation infarctions seen on MRI. 3. No additional vascular abnormality. The right vertebral artery dissection is the likely etiology of the right PICA territory stroke.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Eccentric luminal narrowing with intimal flap and mural thrombus at V3 segment, classic location for spontaneous or trauma-related dissection
Patient age 35 without vascular risk factors makes atherosclerosis unlikely; morphology of narrowing with intimal flap is characteristic of dissection

recommended_actions: Initiate anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy for vertebral artery dissection
Vessel wall MRI for further characterization of intramural hematoma
Follow-up vascular imaging in 3-6 months to assess for recanalization
Evaluate for underlying connective tissue disorder

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Trace mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, physiologic
Mobile interatrial septum with positive bubble study: >20 microbubbles crossing to the left atrium within 3 cardiac cycles at rest, increasing with Valsalva maneuver
Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.clinical_significance: Large patent foramen ovale with significant right-to-left shunt, potential conduit for paradoxical embolism

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram with agitated saline bubble study demonstrates normal biventricular size and function with LVEF of 62%. The notable finding is a large patent foramen ovale with significant right-to-left shunting, with greater than 20 microbubbles crossing into the left atrium within 3 cardiac cycles, increasing with Valsalva. No intracardiac thrombus, vegetation, or structural valve disease. In the context of this young patient's cryptogenic posterior circulation stroke, elevated D-dimer, and recent long-haul flight, this large PFO represents a plausible embolic conduit via paradoxical embolism.
abnormal_values_summary: Large PFO with significant right-to-left shunt (>20 bubbles within 3 cardiac cycles)

findings: Normal sinus rhythm maintained throughout the 72-hour recording
No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular arrhythmia detected
Average heart rate 72 bpm (range 52-118 bpm, appropriate sinus variation)
No significant pauses (longest RR interval 1.42 seconds during sleep)
Rare isolated premature atrial complexes (28 in 72 hours, <0.1% burden)
output.interpretation: Seventy-two hour continuous cardiac monitoring reveals normal sinus rhythm throughout with no atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia. Rare isolated PACs without clinical significance. No arrhythmogenic source identified for cardioembolic stroke.
output.clinical_correlation: The absence of atrial fibrillation on prolonged monitoring, combined with the patient's young age and normal cardiac structure, makes a primary arrhythmogenic cardioembolic mechanism unlikely. The stroke etiology is more likely related to the vertebral artery dissection with possible contribution from paradoxical embolism via the documented PFO.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden increases thrombotic risk 3-8 fold; may have contributed to DVT and paradoxical embolism via PFO, and possibly to in-situ thrombosis at the dissection site

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability workup reveals heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation, a hereditary thrombophilia that increases the risk of venous thromboembolism 3-8 fold. All other thrombophilia markers are within normal limits. In the setting of a young patient with posterior circulation stroke, large PFO, elevated D-dimer, and recent prolonged immobilization during air travel, the Factor V Leiden mutation supports a pathophysiologic model of travel-related deep venous thrombosis with paradoxical embolism through the PFO. This thrombophilia may also contribute to the propensity for in-situ thrombosis at the vertebral artery dissection site.
abnormal_values_summary: Factor V Leiden mutation: Heterozygous positive (hereditary thrombophilia)

findings.location: Bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries

additional_observations: No carotid stenosis or plaque identified bilaterally
Normal flow velocities throughout the bilateral carotid systems

Bilateral vertebral arteries assessed: left vertebral artery is patent with normal antegrade flow; right vertebral artery demonstrates dampened waveform with reduced peak systolic velocity (32 cm/s vs 58 cm/s on the left), consistent with proximal or mid-segment flow-limiting abnormality
output.impression: 1. Normal bilateral carotid arteries without stenosis or plaque, consistent with the patient's young age and absence of atherosclerotic risk factors. 2. Asymmetric vertebral artery flow with dampened waveform on the right, consistent with the known right vertebral artery dissection identified on CTA. 3. No carotid dissection.
recommended_actions: Carotid imaging does not reveal the stroke mechanism; posterior circulation evaluation is the priority
Continue management directed at vertebral artery dissection

Lower Extremity Venous Duplex Ultrasound.value: Acute thrombus identified, non-compressible segment over approximately 4 cm
Lower Extremity Venous Duplex Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Acute deep venous thrombosis in the left peroneal vein, a distal DVT that in the context of a large PFO represents a plausible source for paradoxical embolism

output.interpretation: Lower extremity venous duplex ultrasound reveals an acute deep venous thrombosis in the left peroneal vein with a non-compressible 4 cm segment. All other deep veins are patent and compressible bilaterally. This finding is clinically significant: in a young patient with a large PFO, heterozygous Factor V Leiden, and recent long-haul air travel, this distal DVT represents a highly plausible source for paradoxical embolism resulting in the posterior circulation stroke. The combination of travel-related venous stasis, hereditary thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden), and a large PFO creates a complete pathophysiologic chain for paradoxical embolic stroke.
abnormal_values_summary: Left peroneal vein: Acute DVT, 4 cm non-compressible segment

output.query: young adult posterior circulation stroke vertebral artery dissection patent foramen ovale management

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection in Stroke Study (CADISS): Antiplatelet vs Anticoagulation
results.authors: Markus HS, Hayter E, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for prevention of recurrent stroke in cervical artery dissection. Both are acceptable treatment options.

results.title: Patent Foramen Ovale Closure or Anticoagulation vs Antiplatelets after Stroke (CLOSE Trial)
results.authors: Mas JL, Derumeaux G, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure plus antiplatelet therapy was superior to antiplatelet therapy alone in preventing recurrent stroke in patients aged 16-60 with cryptogenic stroke and PFO with associated atrial septal aneurysm or large shunt.

results.title: Long-Term Outcomes of PFO Closure (RESPECT Extended Follow-up)
results.authors: Saver JL, Carroll JD, et al.
results.key_finding: PFO closure was associated with a lower rate of recurrent ischemic stroke than medical therapy alone over extended follow-up in patients with cryptogenic stroke.

output.summary: In young adults with stroke and vertebral artery dissection, both antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy are acceptable per the CADISS trial. When a large PFO is identified as a contributing factor, PFO closure should be considered per the CLOSE and RESPECT trials, particularly with large shunt or atrial septal aneurysm. In this patient with dual mechanisms (dissection + PFO with DVT), anticoagulation is preferred as it addresses both the dissection-related thrombosis and the venous thromboembolism/paradoxical embolism risk. PFO closure should be considered after the acute phase for long-term secondary prevention.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, right PICA territory (lateral medullary and inferior cerebellar infarction), due to right vertebral artery dissection with possible contribution from paradoxical embolism via patent foramen ovale

differential.likelihood: Moderate initial likelihood, excluded by exam and imaging
differential.key_distinguishing: Peripheral vestibular lesions produce a positive head impulse test (corrective saccade), unidirectional nystagmus that suppresses with fixation, and no skew deviation. This patient has a negative HIT, direction-changing nystagmus, and positive skew — all central signs (HINTS exam dangerous pattern). DWI confirms infarction.

Patient has migraine history but prior episodes were without aura. Basilar migraine typically involves fully reversible brainstem symptoms and should not produce DWI restriction. Persistent deficits and imaging findings exclude migraine.

Acute demyelination can cause brainstem syndromes in young adults. However, the lesion distribution conforms to a vascular territory (PICA), there are no periventricular or juxtacortical lesions typical of MS, and CTA confirms vertebral artery dissection as the mechanism.

PRES typically involves bilateral parieto-occipital regions with vasogenic edema (no DWI restriction), and occurs in the setting of hypertensive emergency, eclampsia, or immunosuppression. None of these apply here.
MRI shows no hemorrhage. DWI pattern confirms ischemic rather than hemorrhagic etiology.

optimal_actions.action: Perform rapid triage with stroke code activation despite atypical presentation — vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia in a young patient with acute onset should trigger posterior circulation stroke evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 4, acute onset symptoms with central vestibular signs

optimal_actions.action: Perform HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) to differentiate central from peripheral cause of acute vestibular syndrome
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dangerous pattern: HIT negative, nystagmus direction-changing, skew deviation present — highly suggestive of central (stroke) etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI (preferred over CT in posterior fossa, where CT sensitivity is limited)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute DWI restriction in right lateral medulla and right inferior cerebellar hemisphere (PICA territory)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, D-dimer, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated D-dimer at 1.42 mg/L FEU; otherwise normal labs; no traditional vascular risk factors
Normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmia — cryptogenic stroke workup needed

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate vertebral arteries for dissection given young patient with posterior circulation stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right vertebral artery dissection at V3 segment with 70% stenosis and intimal flap

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anticoagulation with IV heparin followed by transition to warfarin or DOAC for vertebral artery dissection management
Order transthoracic echocardiogram with agitated saline bubble study to evaluate for PFO given young cryptogenic stroke
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large PFO with >20 bubbles crossing within 3 cardiac cycles

optimal_actions.action: Order lower extremity venous duplex ultrasound given elevated D-dimer and recent long-haul travel to evaluate for DVT as source of paradoxical embolism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute DVT in left peroneal vein

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability workup given young age, DVT, and stroke without traditional risk factors
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring (72-hour Holter or insertable cardiac monitor) to evaluate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No arrhythmia detected — arrhythmogenic mechanism excluded

optimal_actions.action: Plan for PFO closure after acute phase given large shunt, DVT, and cryptogenic stroke, per CLOSE/RESPECT trial data
Admit to stroke unit for close neurological monitoring including serial assessments for cerebellar edema and hydrocephalus

critical_actions: Recognize that acute vestibular syndrome with central signs (negative HIT, direction-changing nystagmus, skew deviation) requires urgent stroke evaluation, not discharge with vestibular neuritis diagnosis
Perform HINTS exam to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo — this bedside exam has higher sensitivity than early CT for posterior circulation stroke
Obtain brain MRI with DWI rather than relying on CT, which has poor sensitivity for posterior fossa infarction especially in the early time window
Perform CTA to identify vertebral artery dissection as the stroke mechanism
Initiate anticoagulation for vertebral artery dissection
Evaluate for PFO with bubble study echocardiogram in a young patient with cryptogenic-appearing stroke
Monitor for posterior fossa complications (cerebellar edema, hydrocephalus, brainstem compression)
contraindicated_actions: Discharging the patient with a diagnosis of vestibular neuritis or benign positional vertigo without neuroimaging — this presentation has central vestibular signs mandating stroke evaluation
Relying on non-contrast CT head alone to exclude posterior circulation stroke — CT has sensitivity as low as 16% for posterior fossa infarction in the first 24 hours
Prescribing meclizine or vestibular suppressants and discharging without stroke workup
Administering IV alteplase without confirming ischemic stroke on imaging — low NIHSS and uncertain diagnosis at presentation could lead to harm if the diagnosis were incorrect
Performing lumbar puncture in a patient with acute cerebellar infarction (risk of herniation if cerebellar edema develops)
Attributing symptoms to migraine in a patient with acute onset, persistent deficits, and no prior aura history
key_reasoning_points: Acute vestibular syndrome (vertigo + nystagmus + gait instability) in a young patient is a common presentation of posterior circulation stroke that is frequently misdiagnosed as peripheral vertigo — up to 35% of posterior circulation strokes present with isolated vestibular symptoms

The HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) is a critical bedside tool: a 'dangerous' pattern (negative HIT, direction-changing nystagmus, skew deviation) has >96% sensitivity for stroke, outperforming early MRI

The crossed sensory findings (ipsilateral facial and contralateral body sensory loss) are pathognomonic for lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome), localizing the lesion to the lateral medulla
Right internuclear ophthalmoplegia and direction-changing nystagmus indicate additional brainstem involvement beyond the classic Wallenberg distribution
Vertebral artery dissection is the most common cause of posterior circulation stroke in young adults, particularly at the V3 segment which is vulnerable to mechanical stress

The combination of Factor V Leiden heterozygosity, recent long-haul flight (venous stasis), and large PFO creates a complete pathophysiologic chain for paradoxical embolism — this may have contributed alongside or independent of the vertebral artery dissection
The elevated D-dimer, while nonspecific, serves as an important clue prompting evaluation for both venous thromboembolism and arterial dissection in the appropriate clinical context

This case has dual stroke mechanisms (vertebral artery dissection AND paradoxical embolism via PFO with DVT), adding to the diagnostic complexity — the relative contribution of each is uncertain and both must be addressed in the treatment plan

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle - Young patient (35yo) with acute vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia mimicking vestibular neuritis. No traditional vascular risk factors. Posterior circulation stroke due to vertebral artery dissection with dual mechanism including paradoxical embolism via PFO. Requires HINTS exam expertise, posterior fossa MRI, and comprehensive young stroke workup.
clinical_pearls: The HINTS exam is more sensitive than early MRI for detecting posterior circulation stroke presenting as acute vestibular syndrome — a dangerous HINTS pattern (negative HIT, direction-changing nystagmus, skew deviation) has >96% sensitivity and >80% specificity for central cause
Posterior fossa CT has sensitivity as low as 7-16% for acute cerebellar and brainstem infarction — MRI with DWI is essential and should not be deferred

Vertebral artery dissection accounts for approximately 25% of posterior circulation strokes in patients under 45 and can occur spontaneously or with minor trauma including chiropractic manipulation, sports, or even vigorous coughing

The triad of vertigo, ipsilateral Horner syndrome, and crossed sensory findings (lateral medullary syndrome) should immediately raise suspicion for PICA territory stroke, though Horner syndrome is absent in approximately 20% of lateral medullary infarctions
PFO closure is now recommended (Class I) for patients aged 18-60 with cryptogenic stroke and high-risk PFO features (large shunt, atrial septal aneurysm), based on CLOSE, RESPECT, and REDUCE trials
